summary: The most common symptom of aortic-iliac occlusive disease is proximal claudication affecting the buttocks or thighs, which results from severe arterial blockage at higher levels compared to peripheral artery disease involving lower extremities. This distinguishes it clinically from other forms like distal PAD where calf pain predominates.
